meta.title: E91 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 3 Series E91 engine — N43, N46, N52, N53, N54, N55, M47, N47, M57, N57, S65. Real UK costs, failure data, and honest advice. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 3 Series E91 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E91 engine typically costs £1,800–£14,000 supply only, depending on engine — the four-cylinder petrol sits at £1,800–£3,800, while the S65 V8 in the M3 can reach £8,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £520–£1,300. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 3 Series E91 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M57 3.0-litre six-cylinder diesel (pre-LCI 325d/330d/335d, 2005–2008) is the most dependable E91 engine — a cast-iron-block six with a genuine 300,000-mile reputation and none of the rear-chain risk that defines the later N47 and N57. The N52 petrol six is the best naturally aspirated option.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 3 Series E91?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain wear on the N47D20 diesel, HPFP and injector failure on the N54 335i, thermostat and swirl flap wear on the M57D30, water pump failure on the N52, and — unrelated to the engine — rear subframe mount cracking on high-mileage cars.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 3 Series E91 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine and which side of the September 2008 facelift your car sits on. Pre-LCI 330d buyers get the bulletproof M57; post-LCI buyers get the improved but chain-affected N57. The N47 diesel four carries this generation's biggest risk regardless of facelift.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 3 Series E91 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a pre-2013 N47D20 without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A neglected N54 335i without HPFP history is the second. The 320si (N45) is a rare homologation special with expensive parts.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E91 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle (N47/N57), long cranking or limp mode (N54 HPFP), overheating with no obvious external leak (N52 water pump), or blue smoke and rough idle (any high-mileage engine) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 3 Series E91 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £8,000 E91, a £4,000 N47 recon is 50% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon is 100% — walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line. On an M3, the calculus is different — the car's value justifies the S65's high replacement cost.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 3 Series E91 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M57 diesel six can exceed 300,000 miles with proper servicing. The N52 petrol six is similarly durable. The N47 and N57's limiting factor is the timing chain — addressed proactively, either can comfortably reach 150,000+ miles. The S65 V8 in the M3 is robust but requires rod bearing replacement as a preventative measure.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E91 and the E90?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E91 is the Touring (estate) variant of the fifth-generation 3 Series (2005–2012), offering a 5-door load-lugging body with the same engine range and platform as the E90 saloon. The E90 is the 4-door saloon (2005–2011), the E92 is the 2-door coupé (2006–2013), and the E93 is the 2-door convertible (2007–2013). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 3 Series E91 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on N47/M57/N57 units. Tell us your variant (318i, 320d, 330d, 335i, M3, etc.) and approximate registration date (pre- or post-September 2008) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 3 Series E91 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E91 Touring — from the N47 four-cylinder diesel to the N54 twin-turbo six and the M57 diesel-six that defined this generation. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the N47 timing chain risk that every estate buyer must know.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 3 Series E91 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E91 Touring — from the four-cylinder petrol and diesel units to the six-cylinder N52 and the twin-turbo N54.

overview.intro: The E91 is the Touring (estate) variant of BMW's fifth-generation 3 Series, launched in September 2005 alongside the E90 saloon. Built on the same platform as its saloon sibling, the E91 offers the same engine range, 50:50 weight distribution, and rear-wheel-drive dynamics — with the added practicality of a 5-door estate body. Production ran until June 2012.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E9x (shared with E90, E92, E93) • Predecessor: BMW 3 Series E46 Touring • Successor: BMW 3 Series F31 Touring • Sibling body styles in this generation: E90 (saloon), E92 (coupé), E93 (convertible)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: As a lower-volume Touring variant, the E91 is a niche enthusiast vehicle — the 330d and 335d remain the most sought-after, while the 320d is the most practical daily driver.

items.quote: The E91's engine range spans three distinct eras. The M57 3.0-litre six-cylinder diesel (pre-LCI, 2005–2008) is the most dependable engine in the range — cast-iron block, proven common-rail injection, and a documented reputation for exceeding 300,000 miles with routine servicing. The N52 3.0-litre naturally aspirated six is the best petrol option — smooth, linear power, no turbo complexity, and none of the HPFP worries that define the N54. The N47 2.0-litre diesel is the most economical choice but carries this generation's defining risk: the rear-mounted timing chain can stretch and fail catastrophically. If you're buying an E91, the M57 six is the sensible choice; the N52 is the petrol sweet spot; the N47 requires documented chain history.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol — reliable, smooth, no turbo worries
High Risk — fast but expensive to maintain
Best Turbo — N54's successor, more reliable
Risk vs Reward — chain history essential
Best Diesel — the M57 is bulletproof

economicsRule.title: The E91 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£5,000 N47 recon against a £5,000 E91 sits at 50–100% of the car's value — right at our 70% BMW threshold. Against a well-kept £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to 31–63% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Against a sub-£4,000 E91, a major engine failure is a write-off. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E91 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M57 330d — cheap thermostat fixes, no chain question.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E91 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: N54 335i — HPFP and turbo repairs add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E91 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M57 330d — six-cylinder torque without the modern diesel's caveats.

problems.title: N47 Diesel Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The N47's timing chain is mounted at the rear of the engine, making it a major labour-intensive job to replace. The chain, guides, and tensioners can wear prematurely, causing chain stretch. If the chain stretches too far, it can "jump" a tooth or snap, causing pistons to strike open valves. Cold-start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle on an N47 diesel E91, get the timing chain inspected immediately. A £1,000–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£5,000 replacement that follows if ignored. On an E91 worth under £5,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

cta.label: Compare N47 engine prices for your E91 →

problems.title: N54 HPFP & Injector Failure
problems.rootCause: The high-pressure fuel pump on the N54 is a known weak point — internal wear leads to fuel pressure loss, hard starting, and eventual engine damage. Injector issues frequently follow, with the injectors failing to seal properly and causing fuel to wash the cylinder walls.
problems.recommendation: If your E91 335i has no documented HPFP history, treat it as overdue preventative maintenance. £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £3,500+ replacement that follows a lean-running failure.

cta.label: Compare N54 engine prices for your E91 335i →

problems.title: M57 Thermostat & Swirl Flap Wear
problems.rootCause: Plastic thermostat housings crack with age and heat cycling; swirl flap linkages in the intake wear and can, in rare cases, detach into the cylinder.
problems.recommendation: This is cheap insurance on the E91's most dependable engine — a £200–£400 thermostat and swirl flap check every service is trivial against the M57's otherwise bulletproof reputation.

cta.label: Compare M57 engine prices for your E91 330d →

problems.title: N52 Water Pump & VANOS Solenoids
problems.rootCause: The N52's electric water pump has a finite service life and can fail without warning, leading to overheating. The VANOS solenoids can also become clogged or fail, causing rough idle, reduced power, and check engine lights.
problems.recommendation: Replace the water pump proactively at 80,000 miles on any N52-powered E91. A £400–£800 preventative job is far cheaper than a £2,500+ engine replacement. Clean the VANOS solenoids at the same time.

cta.label: Compare N52 engine prices for your E91 →
